topic.what: comparative evaluation of four AI agent security guardrails for detecting agent-specific threats and harmful content
topic.how: human-annotated adversarial dataset (1018 samples) testing recall and true negative rates across instruction override, indirect injection, tool abuse, and harmful content categories
topic.why_matters: AI agents face expanding attack surface with instruction override, indirect injection, and tool abuse threats requiring effective guardrail detection

characteristics.what_it_measures: detection rate of malicious/attacker inputs across 852 blocked samples
characteristics.context: tested on 1018 samples from 8 adversarial datasets

characteristics.what_it_measures: correct allowance rate of benign inputs across 166 allowed samples
characteristics.context: high-ambiguity boundary samples from adversarial datasets

characteristics.what_it_measures: rate of benign samples incorrectly blocked

characteristics.what_itasures: range of agent-specific threat categories evaluated
characteristics.value: 8 adversarial datasets covering instruction override, indirect injection, tool abuse, privacy leakage, role hijacking, chain-of-thought poisoning, hate speech, pornography, violence
characteristics.context: ALERT, Salad-Data, Tensor-Trust, PromptWall-Injection, CSSBench, UltraSafety, ToxicQAFinal, Jailbreak-Prompt-Injection

novelty: first comparative benchmark of agent-specific security guardrails
open_problems: reducing false positive rate on high-ambiguity boundary samples (still ~10%)
improving classification precision while maintaining high recall
addressing inadequate default security configurations in agent deployments
